ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for onion pakora; 'fritter (food product)' is the closest parent category covering batter-coated deep-fried foods.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fritters and similar battered fried products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for onion pakora; A0CKP (fritters and similar battered fried products) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for onion pakora or pakora as a food product category.

allergens: gluten (chickpea flour may be cross-contaminated; some recipes use wheat flour)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of onion pakora consists of approximately 4 to 5 pieces, weighing around 100 g, often served as a starter or snack with chutney.

nutrient_source.title: Pakora, onion — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) reports values for onion pakora / besan-battered fried onion snack. Values are adapted from the closest matching entry for besan-based deep-fried onion fritters. Sodium estimated from typical recipe salt content as ICMR-NIN reports sodium in limited entries; cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entry for similar fried chickpea-flour snacks.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chickpea flour-based fried snack (pakora-type); analogous estimate from besan (chickpea flour) GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for onion pakora exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 52 based on the GI of chickpea flour (besan) as the dominant carbohydrate source (GI ~45–55 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for legume-flour products), adjusted upward slightly for deep-frying and the presence of onion. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (52 × 32.8) / 100 = 17.1.

hypertension.guidance: Onion pakora contains approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g from added salt; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as tamarind chutney with added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index and high fat content from deep-frying
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 52 (low-moderate) and 32.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, onion pakora produces a glycemic load of ~17 per serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion size and pair with a low-GI accompaniment to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat and energy density from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Onion pakora is energy-dense at ~298 kcal per 100 g with 16.5 g total fat; while saturated fat is relatively low at 1.8 g per 100 g, frequent consumption of deep-fried snacks is associated with increased cardiovascular risk and should be limited to occasional intake for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Onion pakora (also called onion bhaji or pyaaz pakoda) is a popular Indian deep-fried snack made by coating thin slices of onion in a spiced chickpea flour (besan) batter and frying them until golden and crispy. The batter typically includes besan, water, salt, red chilli powder, cumin, coriander, and sometimes ajwain (carom seeds). The result is a crunchy exterior with a soft, slightly sweet onion interior. Onion pakora is a staple street food and home snack across the Indian subcontinent and is widely served in Indian restaurants globally, including in Singapore. It is commonly served as a starter or monsoon-season snack with green chutney or tamarind sauce. Nutritionally, onion pakora is energy-dense (~298 kcal per 100 g) due to deep-frying, with moderate carbohydrate content (32.8 g) primarily from chickpea flour, moderate protein (6.2 g), and significant fat (16.5 g). The chickpea flour base provides dietary fibre (3.2 g) and contributes to a relatively low glycemic index (~52). Clinically, portion control is advised for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its fat content, sodium, and energy density.
common_user_queries: How many calories in onion bhaji?
What is onion pakora made of?
What is the GI of onion pakora?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for besan-based deep-fried onion fritters, which is the closest approved primary source for this Indian dish. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database chickpea flour data. No exact match for onion pakora exists in any single approved source, hence medium confidence.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this food category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this food category. Trans fat is 0.0 as vegetable oil used in deep-frying in Indian commercial kitchens typically contains negligible industrially produced trans fats; confirmed negligible per ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy data for similar fried snacks. Cholesterol is 0.0 as onion pakora contains no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on chickpea flour (besan) GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for onion pakora was found. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (52 × 32.8) / 100 = 17.1 for a 100 g serving. Sodium value of 320 mg per 100 g is estimated from typical recipe salt content and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy data for similar fried chickpea-flour snacks, as ICMR-NIN does not report sodium for all entries. The food is served at Zaffron Tandoori – Boat Quay on field survey data at SGD 12.90 per portion.
